concept: Vomit / The Body's Reversal / Expel from the Stomach
description: To vomit: the stomach's emphatic refusal. Coined plainly, per the language's ruling against squeamishness — medicine and care need the word, and euphemism serves no one at a sickbed.
sound_symbolism: Deep 'mu' rising in reverse through 'pe' to 'lo' — the path backward, said quickly.
grammatical_notes: Intransitive: 'mokela miona mupelo.' (The sick person vomits.) The carer's sentence: 'lu lopia mupelo. no wei mia shane.' (If the child vomits, tell me.)

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Dignity at the sickbed comes from calm words for hard facts; the plain verb is kinder than any euphemism.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: The sickroom's plainest fact, and often its most important one — care runs on unembarrassed reporting.
